dataset: Real SCOTUS cases and published law review articles on Fourth Amendment digital privacy

pi_items.source: Live DB query confirmed 18 items (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM items WHERE libraryID=1 AND itemTypeID NOT IN (1,3,31) = 18). Export JSON also confirms total_items=18. Screenshot shows '18 items in this view'.
DB dump shows 11 rows with itemTypeID=9 (case). Export JSON confirms 11 items with itemTypeID=9.
DB dump shows 7 rows with itemTypeID=24 (journalArticle). Export JSON confirms 7 items with itemTypeID=24.

pi_items.metadata_value: ["Katz v. United States", "United States v. Miller", "Florida v. Jardines"]
pi_items.verified_value: ["Katz v. United States" (F.2d), "United States v. Miller" (F.3d), "Florida v. Jardines" (F.2d)]
pi_items.source: DB dump confirms: Katz reporter=F.2d (itemID=4), Miller reporter=F.3d (itemID=5), Jardines reporter=F.2d (itemID=6). SQL query for fieldID=49 with value IN ('F.2d','F.3d') returns exactly these 3 cases. All are SCOTUS cases that should have reporter='U.S.'

All 11 cases are United States Supreme Court decisions. SCOTUS opinions are reported in United States Reports (U.S.), not Federal Reporter (F.2d/F.3d). Verified via Justia: Katz=389 U.S. 347, Miller=425 U.S. 435, Jardines=569 U.S. 1.

pi_items.metadata_value: ["Mapp v. Ohio", "Smith v. Maryland", "Kyllo v. United States"]
pi_items.verified_value: ["Mapp v. Ohio", "Smith v. Maryland", "Kyllo v. United States"]
pi_items.source: DB dump confirms: Mapp (itemID=1), Smith (itemID=2), Kyllo (itemID=3) all have empty court field (column 5 is blank in pipe-delimited dump). SQL query for items NOT IN (SELECT itemID FROM itemData WHERE fieldID=60) returns exactly these 3. Screenshot of Kyllo metadata pane visually confirms empty court field.

Mapp v. Ohio 367 U.S. 643 (1961), Smith v. Maryland 442 U.S. 735 (1979), Kyllo v. United States 533 U.S. 27 (2001) — all decided by the United States Supreme Court. Verified via Justia, Oyez, and Library of Congress.

DB shows dateDecided=2068-06-10 (planted error: 100 years in future). Terry v. Ohio 392 U.S. 1 was decided June 10, 1968 per Justia (https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/392/1/), Oyez, and Kansas Bureau of Investigation PDF. Correct date is 1968-06-10.

DB shows dateDecided=2114-06-25 (planted error: 100 years in future). Riley v. California 573 U.S. 373 was decided June 25, 2014 per Justia (https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/573/373/) and Wikipedia. Correct date is 2014-06-25.

pi_items.key: wrong_date_items.Cell Phone Location Data and the Fourth Amendment
pi_items.source: DB shows date=2111 (planted error: 100 years in future). Susan Freiwald, 'Cell Phone Location Data and the Fourth Amendment: A Question of Law, Not Fact,' 70 Md. L. Rev. 681 (2011). Published in Maryland Law Review Vol. 70, Iss. 3, 2011. Verified via University of Maryland Digital Commons (https://digitalcommons.law.umaryland.edu/mlr/vol70/iss3/6/) and SSRN.

DB shows firstPage=999 (planted error: placeholder). Illinois v. Gates, 462 U.S. 213 (1983) — first page is 213. Verified via Justia (https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/462/213/), LII Cornell, FindLaw, and Library of Congress.

DB shows volume=54 (planted error: leading digit dropped). Daniel J. Solove, 'A Taxonomy of Privacy,' 154 U. Pa. L. Rev. 477 (2006). Volume 154 verified via UPenn Law Review repository (https://scholarship.law.upenn.edu/penn_law_review/vol154/iss3/1/) and Scientific Research Publishing reference (pp. 477-570).

DB shows volume=5 (planted error: leading digits dropped). Paul Ohm, 'Broken Promises of Privacy,' 57 UCLA L. Rev. 1701 (2010). Volume 57 verified via UCLA Law Review website (https://www.uclalawreview.org/broken-promises-of-privacy-responding-to-the-surprising-failure-of-anonymization-2/) and SSRN.

DB shows pages=97 (planted error: truncated). Stephen E. Henderson, 'Beyond the (Current) Fourth Amendment,' 34 Pepp. L. Rev. 975 (2007). The article was not found via web search (limited online indexing), but the citation format is consistent with Pepperdine Law Review Vol. 34. The page range 975-1036 is consistent with a ~60-page law review article. Marked verified based on setup script provenance (author-created data with realistic citation).

pi_items.metadata_value: Fourth Amendment Digital Privacy > {Foundational Precedent, Digital Privacy Doctrine, Scholarly Analysis, Limiting Authority}
pi_items.verified_value: Foundational Precedent: Mapp (1961), Katz (1967), Terry (1968), Jardines (2013); Digital Privacy Doctrine: Kyllo (2001), Jones (2012), Riley (2014), Carpenter (2018); Limiting Authority: Miller (1976), Smith (1979), Gates (1983); Scholarly Analysis: all 7 articles
pi_items.source: Classification verified against legal doctrine: Foundational cases established core Fourth Amendment privacy principles (exclusionary rule, reasonable expectation of privacy, stop-and-frisk, physical trespass). Digital Privacy cases applied Fourth Amendment to modern technology (thermal imaging, GPS, cell phones, CSLI). Limiting Authority cases narrowed privacy protections (third-party doctrine for bank records, pen registers, totality-of-circumstances for probable cause). All articles are scholarly works belonging in Scholarly Analysis.

pi_items.verified_value: Carpenter-Freiwald (both about cell phone location data), Katz-Kerr (both about Fourth Amendment and new technologies), Smith-Murphy (both about third-party doctrine), Riley-Tokson (both about knowledge and digital privacy)
pi_items.source: Topical pairing verified: Carpenter v. US (2018) held CSLI requires a warrant; Freiwald's article argues the same for cell phone location data. Katz established reasonable expectation of privacy for technology; Kerr's article analyzes Fourth Amendment application to new technologies. Smith established third-party doctrine; Murphy's article is literally titled 'The Case Against the Case for Third-Party Doctrine.' Riley held cell phone searches require warrants; Tokson examines knowledge in Fourth Amendment privacy analysis. All pairings are topically unambiguous.

pi_items.verified_value: jm-indigo (JM Indigo Law Review styles available in Jurism)
pi_items.source: Live testing confirmed: Preferences > Export tab shows Quick Copy dropdown with citation styles. The configure_quick_copy_indigo task in the same environment requires setting this exact style, confirming it is available. Screenshot evidence shows the Export tab with style dropdown.

Live testing confirmed: ga user can write to /home/ga/Documents/ (WRITE_OK). File > Export Library menu item is available in Jurism. RIS is a standard Zotero/Jurism export format. Export result script checks this exact path.

privileged_info_summary: The Juris-M library contains exactly 18 items: 11 Supreme Court cases (itemTypeID=9) and 7 law review articles (itemTypeID=24). There are 13 planted metadata errors the agent must fix. WRONG REPORTERS (3 cases, all should be 'U.S.'): Katz v. United States has reporter 'F.2d', United States v. Miller has reporter 'F.3d', Florida v. Jardines has reporter 'F.2d'. MISSING COURTS (3 cases, all should be 'United States Supreme Court'): Mapp v. Ohio, Smith v. Maryland, and Kyllo v. United States have empty/missing court fields. WRONG DATES (3 items, 100 years in the future): Terry v. Ohio shows 2068-06-10 (correct: 1968-06-10), Riley v. California shows 2114-06-25 (correct: 2014-06-25), and the Freiwald article 'Cell Phone Location Data and the Fourth Amendment' shows date 2111 (correct: 2011). WRONG FIRST PAGE (1 case): Illinois v. Gates shows firstPage 999 (correct: 213). WRONG VOLUMES (2 articles): 'A Taxonomy of Privacy' by Solove shows volume 54 (correct: 154), 'Broken Promises of Privacy' by Ohm shows volume 5 (correct: 57). WRONG PAGES (1 article): 'Beyond the (Current) Fourth Amendment' by Henderson shows pages 97 (correct: 975-1036). A verified reference CSV file is at /home/ga/Documents/correct_citations.csv containing all correct values. The agent must create a parent collection 'Fourth Amendment Digital Privacy' with 4 subcollections: 'Foundational Precedent' (Mapp, Katz, Terry, Jardines), 'Digital Privacy Doctrine' (Kyllo, Jones, Riley, Carpenter), 'Limiting Authority' (Miller, Smith, Gates), and 'Scholarly Analysis' (all 7 articles). The agent must create Related links between 4 case-article pairs: Carpenter with Freiwald, Katz with Kerr, Smith with Murphy, Riley with Tokson. The agent must set Quick Copy style to 'JM Indigo (Law Review)' in Edit > Preferences > Export, and export the entire library as RIS to /home/ga/Documents/symposium_bibliography.ris via File > Export Library. Initial state has 0 collections, 0 relations, 0 tags.
